title: Honeycomb incident
description: On July 25th, 2023, we experienced a total Honeycomb outage. It impacted all user-facing components from 1:40 p.m. UTC to 2:48 p.m. UTC, during which no data could be processed or accessed. The full details of incident triage process is covered in [here](https://www.honeycomb.io/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Incident-Review-What-Comes-Up-Must-First-Go-Down.pdf).
symptoms: Engineers observed a marginally slow, inconsistent performance burn in the ingest service, leading to a total outage where no data could be processed or accessed.
root_cause: A subtle implementation bug caused by a feature flag switch that prevented writes from migrating to the correct cluster, compounded by a failure to restart hosts after the flag was toggled.
resolution: A command was written to restart the query engine’s cluster, but the database seized up before it could be executed, leading to a total system crash.
summary: The incident involved a total outage of Honeycomb services due to a subtle implementation bug related to a feature flag switch, which prevented data writes and led to a system crash. Despite attempts to restart the query engine, the database became unresponsive, resulting in a complete service failure.
content_preview: Incident Review: What Comes Up Must First Go Down | Honeycomb Save the date for O11yCon 2026 Get Early Access On July 25th, 2023, we experienced a total Honeycomb outage. It impacted all user-facing components from 1:40 p.m. UTC to 2:48 p.m. UTC, during which no data could be processed or accessed. This outage is the most severe we’ve had since we had paying customers. In this review, we will cover the incident itself, and then we’ll zoom back out for an analysis of multiple contributing element

title: North American Electric Power System incident
description: A power outage in Ohio around 1600h EDT cascaded up through a web of systemic vulnerabilities and process failures and resulted in an outage in the power grid affecting ~50,000,000 people for ~4 days in some areas, and caused rolling blackouts in Ontario for about a week thereafter.
symptoms: Power outage affecting ~50,000,000 people, rolling blackouts in Ontario.
root_cause: Systemic vulnerabilities and process failures.
resolution: Not specified
duration: Outage lasted for ~4 days in some areas, rolling blackouts for about a week thereafter.
summary: A power outage in Ohio due to systemic vulnerabilities and process failures affected ~50,000,000 people for ~4 days, leading to rolling blackouts in Ontario for about a week. The incident highlights significant weaknesses in the power grid's infrastructure.

title: Heroku incident
description: An upgrade silently disabled a check that was meant to prevent filesystem corruption in running containers. A subsequent deploy caused filesystem corruption in running containers.
symptoms: Filesystem corruption in running containers observed after a deploy.
root_cause: An upgrade silently disabled a check meant to prevent filesystem corruption.
resolution: Not specified
summary: After a deploy, engineers observed filesystem corruption in running containers due to an upgrade that disabled a critical check. The incident highlights the importance of maintaining checks to prevent such issues.
content_preview: Engineering | Heroku Search overlay panel for performing site-wide searches Search For: Close Engineering Behind the Scenes: How Maintaining Cloud Native Buildpacks Powers Platforms Like Heroku Ecosystem , Engineering Last Updated: March 17, 2026 Juan Bustamante , Josh Sullivan Most developers never see the 11 pack releases we shipped in the last 14 months as pack CLI maintainers. That’s actually a good sign—it means the infrastructure just works. When a critical vulnerability emerges requiring

title: Kickstarter incident
description: Primary DB became inconsistent with all replicas, which wasn't detected until a query failed. This was caused by a MySQL bug which sometimes caused `order by` to be ignored.
symptoms: Primary DB became inconsistent with all replicas, which wasn't detected until a query failed. Error message: Duplicate entry '123456-789' for key 'index_backings_on_project_id_and_sequence'.
root_cause: A MySQL bug which sometimes caused 'order by' to be ignored.
resolution: Engineers stabilized the site, minimized effects of stale replicas, communicated the issue to users, and recovered.
summary: Kickstarter experienced a critical incident where the primary database became inconsistent with all replicas due to a MySQL bug. Engineers stabilized the site and communicated with users while recovering from the issue.
content_preview: The Day the Replication Died – Kickstarter Engineering Follow Sign in / Sign up The Day the Replication Died On Thursday, March 7th, we scrambled. Most of Kickstarter’s traffic is served from replicated copies of the MySQL database, and those replicas had quit updating. The problem wasn’t on one replica that we could easily replace; it was on all the replicas. And MySQL was telling us something new: mysql> SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G *************************** 1. row *************************** Last_Er

title: ESLint incident
description: On July 12th, 2018, an attacker compromised the npm account of an ESLint maintainer and published malicious packages to the npm registry.
symptoms: Malicious packages were published to the npm registry, leading to potential exposure of users' .npmrc files.
root_cause: An attacker compromised the npm account of an ESLint maintainer due to password reuse and lack of two-factor authentication.
resolution: The malicious packages were unpublished from the npm registry, and npm revoked all access tokens issued before the incident.
summary: On July 12th, 2018, malicious packages were published to the npm registry after an attacker compromised an ESLint maintainer's account. The incident was resolved by unpublishing the malicious packages and revoking compromised access tokens.
content_preview: Postmortem for Malicious Packages Published on July 12th, 2018 - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter Skip to main content Published 12 Jul, 2018 under Postmortems Postmortem for Malicious Packages Published on July 12th, 2018 Malicious versions of some ESLint packages were published (now unpublished) and we are sorry about this. We share details of the attack and our precautionary recommendations for package maintainers. Please check that you are not using the affected packages. Summary On July

title: GPS/GLONASS incident
description: A bad update that caused incorrect orbital mechanics calculations caused GPS satellites that use GLONASS to broadcast incorrect positions for 10 hours. The bug was noticed and rolled back almost immediately due to (?) this didn't fix the issue.
symptoms: GPS satellites broadcasted incorrect positions for 10 hours.
root_cause: A bad update caused incorrect orbital mechanics calculations.
resolution: The bug was noticed and rolled back almost immediately, but this didn't fix the issue.
summary: A bad update led to incorrect orbital mechanics calculations, causing GPS satellites to broadcast incorrect positions for 10 hours. The issue was identified and rolled back quickly, but the rollback did not resolve the problem.

title: NASA incident
description: A design flaw in the Apollo 11 rendezvous radar produced excess CPU load, causing the spacecraft computer to restart during lunar landing.
symptoms: Five program alarms and software restarts during lunar landing.
root_cause: A design flaw in the Apollo 11 rendezvous radar interface that stole approximately 13% of the computer's duty cycle.
resolution: Not specified
summary: During the Apollo 11 lunar landing, the spacecraft experienced five program alarms and software restarts due to a design flaw in the rendezvous radar interface, which consumed 13% of the computer's duty cycle. The incident highlights critical issues in the spacecraft's hardware design.

title: TravisCI incident
description: A configuration issue (automated age-based Google Compute Engine VM image cleanup job) caused stable base VM images to be deleted.
symptoms: Build jobs stopped running due to the deletion of stable base VM images.
root_cause: An automated cleanup service deleted stable VM images because they were not included in the internal image catalog due to a hard-coded limit on the number of image names queried.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The incident involved the deletion of stable base VM images, which caused build jobs to stop running. The root cause was a configuration issue in the automated cleanup service that failed to recognize stable images. Resolution steps were not specified.
content_preview: The Travis CI Blog: The day we deleted our VM images On August 9, we had a major incident on our Google Compute Engine setup. This was something more than just an outage. Our system wasnât just down for an extended period of time, we permanently lost our VM images used for builds running on GCE. We are deeply sorry for the outage and especially all the trouble the aftermath has caused our users. Now that some time has passed, we would like to take another look at what happened and give you som

title: Duo incident
description: Cascading failure due to a request queue overloading the existing, insufficient database capacity. Inadequate capacity planning and monitoring could be attributed as well.
symptoms: Increased authentication latency and intermittent request timeouts for all customer applications protected by the Duo service.
root_cause: Cascading failure due to a request queue overloading the existing, insufficient database capacity.
resolution: Flushed the queued requests on each application server to stabilize the platform.
summary: The DUO1 deployment experienced increased authentication latency and request timeouts due to a cascading failure from overloaded request queues. The issue was resolved by flushing the queued requests on application servers.

The root cause of this and the previ

title: Honeycomb incident
description: On September 8th, 2022, our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. We will first cover the background behind the incident with a high-level view of the relevant architecture, how we tried to investigate and fix the system, and finally, we’ll go over some meaningful elements that surfaced from our incident review process.
symptoms: Shepherd hosts were running out of memory (OOM) and restarting, leading to cascading crashes in the Refinery cluster. Engineers observed a persistent, metastable failure loop and OOM errors recurred in both Shepherd and Refinery.
root_cause: A failure in the Refinery cluster caused Shepherd to accumulate and queue up data, which led to running out of space and being killed.
resolution: Engineers slowed rollouts to stabilize the system, implemented aggressive sampling to reduce data in the Refinery cluster, and pinned builds to prevent further deployments.
summary: The incident involved persistent OOM errors in Shepherd hosts due to a failure in the Refinery cluster, which caused data accumulation. Engineers stabilized the system by slowing rollouts and implementing aggressive sampling.
content_preview: Incident Review: Shepherd Cache Delays | Honeycomb Save the date for O11yCon 2026 Get Early Access In this incident review, we’ll cover the outage from September 8th, 2022, where our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. We will first cover the background behind the incident with a high-level view of the relevant architecture, how we tried to investigate and fix the system, and finally, we’ll go over some meaningful elements that surfaced from our inci

title: Therac-25 incident
description: The Therac-25 was a radiation therapy machine involved in at least six accidents between 1985 and 1987 in which patients were given massive overdoses of radiation. Because of concurrent programming errors, it sometimes gave its patients radiation doses that were thousands of times greater than normal, resulting in death or serious injury.
symptoms: Patients received massive overdoses of radiation, resulting in death or serious injury.
root_cause: Concurrent programming errors in the Therac-25 software.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Therac-25 radiation therapy machine experienced concurrent programming errors that led to patients receiving overdoses of radiation, causing fatalities and serious injuries. The incident highlights the critical need for robust software testing and error handling in medical devices.

title: Foursquare incident
description: MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory. The failure was catastrophic and not graceful due to a a query pattern that involved a read-load with low levels of locality (each user check-in caused a read of all check-ins for the user's history, and records were 300 bytes with no spatial locality, meaning that most of the data pulled in from each page was unnecessary). A lack of monitoring on the MongoDB instances caused the high load to go undetected until the load became catastrophic, causing 17 hours of downtime spanning two incidents in two days.
symptoms: The system experienced a catastrophic failure with 17 hours of downtime, and initial outage reports were blaming MongoDB or stating the problem was unknown.
root_cause: MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory due to a query pattern with low levels of locality and a lack of monitoring on the MongoDB instances.
resolution: Not specified
summary: Foursquare experienced a critical outage lasting 17 hours due to MongoDB running out of memory from a poor query pattern and lack of monitoring, leading to a catastrophic failure.

title: Keepthescore incident
description: Engineers deleted the production database by accident. Database is a managed database from DigitalOcean with backups once a day. 30 minutes after the disaster, it went back online, however 7 hours of scoreboard data was gone forever.
symptoms: Production database was deleted, resulting in the loss of over 300,000 scoreboards and their associated data.
root_cause: A function intended to delete the local database mistakenly connected to the production database and wiped it.
resolution: The website was taken into maintenance mode, and a backup was restored 30 minutes after the incident.
duration: 30 minutes to restore the website, but 7 hours of scoreboard data was lost forever.
summary: Engineers accidentally deleted the production database, losing over 300,000 scoreboards due to a misconfigured function. The website was restored from a backup 30 minutes later, but 7 hours of data was irretrievably lost.
content_preview: We deleted the production database by accident ð¥ | Keep the Score blog Today at around 10:45pm CET, after a couple of glasses of red wine, we deleted the production database by accident ð¨. Over 300.00 scoreboards and their associated data were vaporised in an instant. Thankfully our database is a managed database from DigitalOcean, which means that DigitalOcean automatically do backups once a day. After 5 minutes of hand-wringing and panic, we took the website into maintenance mode and wor

title: Bungie incident
description: Side effects of a bug fix for wrong timestamps causes data loss; server misconfiguration for the hotfix causes the data loss to reappear in several servers in a following update.
symptoms: A small percentage of players lost currency and materials, leading to player account rollbacks.
root_cause: A game bug involving inventory management and server misconfigurations that re-introduced the bug after it was fixed.
resolution: Accounts were rolled back to the state they were in before the launch of Hotfix 2.7.1.1.
summary: Players experienced data loss of currency and materials due to a bug in inventory management and server misconfigurations. The issue was resolved by rolling back accounts to a previous state.
content_preview: Destiny 2 Outage and Rollback > News | Bungie.net JavaScript is required to use Bungie.net Destiny 2 Outage and Rollback Feb 12, 2020 - Destiny Dev Team Earlier today (Tuesday, February 11th), after the launch of Hotfix 2.7.1.1, we were made aware of the re-emergence of an issue which caused a small percentage of players to lose currency and materials. This comes after the first incidence of this issue, which caused all players to lose currency and materials on January 28th with the launch of Ho

title: Sun incident
description: Sun famously didn't include ECC in a couple generations of server parts. This resulted in data corruption and crashing. Following Sun's typical MO, they made customers that reported a bug sign an NDA before explaining the issue.
symptoms: High-end servers made by Sun Microsystems suddenly crashed, affecting major corporate accounts like America Online and Ebay.
root_cause: An odd problem involving stray cosmic rays and memory chips in the flagship Enterprise server line.
resolution: Sun will make repairs on a one-to-one basis with customers who have had a problem, but will not fix all sold servers.
summary: Sun Microsystems' high-end servers experienced crashes due to cosmic rays affecting memory chips, leading to significant issues for major clients. The company is addressing the problem on a case-by-case basis without a full recall.
content_preview: Sun Screen Sun Screen Nov 13, 2000, 12:00am EST Jun 06, 2013, 12:50pm EDT This article is more than 10 years old. T HE MYSTERIOUS GLITCH has been popping up since late last year. At a new Web company in San Francisco, a telecommunications company in the Midwest, a Baby Bell in Atlanta, anInternet domain registry on the East Coast--for no apparent reason, high-end servers made by Sun Microsystems suddenly crashed. It happens not to all of them, and not all the time, but enough to cause problems f

title: Cloudflare incident
description: A CPU exhaustion was caused by a single WAF rule that contained a poorly written regular expression that ended up creating excessive backtracking. This rule was deployed quickly to production and a series of events lead to a global 27 minutes downtime of the Cloudflare services.
symptoms: Customers experienced 502 error pages when visiting any Cloudflare domain.
root_cause: A poorly written regular expression in a WAF rule caused excessive backtracking, leading to CPU exhaustion.
resolution: The problematic WAF rule was identified and removed to restore normal service.
summary: A critical incident at Cloudflare on July 2, 2019, resulted in 27 minutes of downtime due to a poorly written WAF rule that caused CPU exhaustion, leading to widespread 502 errors. The issue was resolved by removing the problematic rule.
content_preview: Details of the Cloudflare outage on July 2, 2019 Details of the Cloudflare outage on July 2, 2019 Loading... July 12, 2019 4:45PM John Graham-Cumming Almost nine years ago, Cloudflare was a tiny company and I was a customer not an employee. Cloudflare had launched a month earlier and one day alerting told me that my little site, jgc.org , didn’t seem to have working DNS any more. Cloudflare had pushed out a change to its use of Protocol Buffers and it had broken DNS. I wrote to Matthew Prince di

title: CrowdStrike incident
description: A Content update containing undetected errors was deployed due to a bug in the Content Validator in the deployment stage. This problematic content caused an out-of-bounds memory read, resulting in a Windows operating system crash (BSOD) on 8.5 million Windows machines. The update was reverted within 78 minutes, but the incident highlighted the need for improved validation and testing processes.
symptoms: Out-of-bounds memory read caused Windows operating system crashes (BSOD) on 8.5 million machines.
root_cause: A bug in the Content Validator during the deployment stage led to undetected errors in the content update.
resolution: The problematic content update was reverted within 78 minutes.
summary: A content update deployed by CrowdStrike resulted in out-of-bounds memory reads, causing BSOD on 8.5 million Windows machines. The root cause was a bug in the Content Validator, and the issue was resolved by reverting the update within 78 minutes.
content_preview: Falcon Content Update Remediation and Guidance Hub | CrowdStrike Remediation and Guidance Hub: Channel File 291 Incident Support Portal Translated resources Page last updated 2024-08-06 2119 UTC Updated 2024-07-31 1638 UTC (Final Post-Incident Measurement Report) Using a week-over-week comparison, ~99% of Windows sensors are online as of July 29 at 5pm PT, compared to before the content update. We typically see a variance of ~1% week-over-week in sensor connections. Updated 2024-07-25 1954 UTC U

title: Amazon incident
description: At 10:25pm PDT on June 4, loss of power at an AWS Sydney facility resulting from severe weather in that area lead to disruption to a significant number of instances in an Availability Zone. Due to the signature of the power loss, power  isolation breakers did not engage, resulting in backup energy reserves draining into the degraded power grid.
symptoms: Loss of power to a significant number of EC2 instances and their associated EBS volumes in an Availability Zone, resulting in service disruption.
root_cause: A loss of power at a regional substation due to severe weather caused an unusually long voltage sag, leading to failure of power isolation breakers and subsequent draining of backup energy reserves.
resolution: Power was manually re-engaged by on-site technicians, and automated systems restored power to the affected infrastructure.
duration: From 10:25 PM PDT on June 4 to 1:00 AM PDT on June 5.
summary: The incident involved a significant loss of power to EC2 instances in an Availability Zone due to a voltage sag caused by severe weather, which led to backup systems failing. Power was restored manually and through automated systems, resolving the disruption.
content_preview: Summary of the AWS Service Event in the Sydney Region Skip to main content Summary of the AWS Service Event in the Sydney Region We’d like to share more detail about the AWS service disruption that occurred this past weekend in the AWS Sydney Region. The service disruption primarily affected EC2 instances and their associated Elastic Block Store (“EBS”) volumes running in a single Availability Zone. Loss of Power At 10:25 PM PDT on June 4th, our utility provider suffered a loss of power at a reg

title: Yeller incident
description: A network partition in a cluster caused some messages to get delayed, up to 6-7 hours. For reasons that aren't clear, a rolling restart of the cluster healed the partition. There's some suspicious that it was due to cached routes, but there wasn't enough logging information to tell for sure.
symptoms: Processing delays on 42 exceptions, with some delays up to 7 hours. Customers could not sign up for new accounts, change passwords, reset passwords, change billing, or invite users to projects.
root_cause: Network partitions in the cluster caused by maintenance at the datacenter.
resolution: A rolling restart of the cluster was performed, which resolved the network partition.
duration: Approximately 17 hours from the start of the incident until resolution.
summary: Yeller experienced severe processing delays due to network partitions caused by datacenter maintenance, impacting exception processing and user account management. A rolling restart of the cluster resolved the issue.

Yeller underwent some severe exception processing delays - in a few cases up to the order of 7 hours. I am very sorry this issue happened, providing a fas

title: Firefox incident
description: On January 13th, 2022, a specific code path in Firefox's network stack triggered a problem in the HTTP/3 protocol implementation. This blocked network communication and made Firefox unresponsive, unable to load web content for nearly two hours.
symptoms: Firefox became unusable for close to two hours, with a spike in crashes reported and user complaints about the browser hanging.
root_cause: A change by Google Cloud Platform made HTTP/3 the default protocol, which triggered a bug in Firefox's network stack.
resolution: HTTP/3 was explicitly disabled on GCP at 09:12 UTC.
summary: On January 13, 2022, Firefox users experienced a nearly two-hour outage due to a bug in the network stack triggered by an unannounced change in HTTP/3 protocol settings by Google Cloud Platform. The issue was resolved by disabling HTTP/3 on GCP.
content_preview: Retrospective and Technical Details on the recent Firefox Outage - Mozilla Hacks - the Web developer blog Retrospective and Technical Details on the recent Firefox Outage By Christian Holler Posted on February 2, 2022 in Featured Article , Firefox , and Firefox OS On January 13th 2022, Firefox became unusable for close to two hours for users worldwide. This incident interrupted many people’s workflow. This post highlights the complex series of events and circumstances that, together, triggered a
